inline_comments.message: just a question - will this prevent systemd-resolved from overwriting the changes made too /etc/resolv.conf.  (I found in my own notes for a recent ubuntu18-04 setup, I changed my dns configuration method from modifying /etc/resolve.conf to /etc/systemd/resolve.conf - due to that concern - i.e. there was a warning message in /etc/resolv.conf about not making changes in it)
Why are you assuming this IP will work as nameserver for anyone? Why not using Vagrant's driver own nameserver facilities?

On Systemd managed systems resolv.conf is a symlink:

/etc/resolv.conf -> ../run/systemd/resolve/stub-resolv.conf

Detaching symlink like above would work but i'd also be for a more Systemd native solution for adding custom nameserver.

Simple shell redirect ">" would look nicer than "| tee" IMO.

This IP is the default one assigned to the second libvirt-managed interface (typically used by vagrant-libvirt plugin). You are correct that it works under certain assumptions:

1) libvirt provider is used for this Vagrant machine
2) vagrant-libvirt uses "192.168.121.1" address for Vagrant machine's host

Those assumptions have been made to support Vagrant + libvirt stack (with vagrant-libvirt plugin) like in minimal-onap [1] without the need to access public DNS servers hardcoded in the base box [2] and using host ones instead. I came across this approach in Ubuntu Vagrant boxes [3], e.g. ubuntu/bionic64. They are built for VirtualBox only, though.

Setting DNS server could have been made virtualization provider-specific step if not for provisioner evaluation order [4] which would postpone this action.

What other solutions are available?

1) Providing new Vagrant base box without hardcoded DNS servers; needs: setup, infra, maintenance
2) Providing out-of-Vagrantfile patch for libvirt support and simplified way of applying it; needs: setup, maintenance
3) Providing quickstart documentation describing verified environment (pending); seemed to be simpler approach to me

Let me know what you think

[1] https://git.onap.org/integration/tree/bootstrap/vagrant-minimal-onap/README.rst
[2] https://github.com/lavabit/robox/blob/806fc9412a9a4164945b106f5f6ae53741d3baa2/scripts/ubuntu1804/network.sh#L44
[3] https://launchpad.net/cloud-images
[4] https://github.com/hashicorp/vagrant/issues/6035

I seem to have missed the fact it's for libvirt driver, i was considering it in scope of virtualbox which i use for default. Thank you for precise explanation.

I'm also for (3), would be good to document the properties of working env in case someone has some uncommon setup.
